title: Geoeconomic Monitor: Changing With The Times
gist: Paywall teaser: oil has fallen to a three-month low despite no US-Iran interim deal, reflecting both truce-expectation trading and the oil market's ability to absorb the Strait of Hormuz supply shock without major global disruption; a second section previews USMCA review scenarios. Light card — intro only.

insights.claim: Oil has dropped to a three-month low despite the US and Iran failing to agree an interim peace deal that would let energy exports resume through the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Tom Holland, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: Crude weakness despite an unresolved geopolitical flashpoint signals a soft demand/supply backdrop — bearish read for energy bulls positioned for a Hormuz premium.
insights.quote: the price of oil has dropped to a three-month low

insights.claim: The longer-term softness in oil reflects the market's ability to absorb the Strait of Hormuz supply shock without major global economic disruption, beyond short-term truce-expectation trading.
insights.speaker: Tom Holland
insights.so_what: Implies the supply-disruption tail risk is overpriced; fade Hormuz-driven oil spikes rather than chase them.
insights.quote: the longer-term softness reflects the oil market's ability in absorbing the Hormuz supply shock without major global economic disruption

insights.claim: Tri-party trade talks are underway ahead of the upcoming USMCA review, with different probable outcomes carrying distinct consequences.
insights.speaker: Tom Miller
insights.so_what: USMCA review outcomes are a live North American trade catalyst; relevant to Mexico/Canada exposure and supply-chain positioning.
